---
title: "Drafting visualizations"
author: "Joaquin Sandoval"
format: html
editor: visual
---

### **III. Some pre-planning**

Restate the questions you hope to answer with your inforgraphic. This should include *one overarching question*(think of this as driving the overall theme of your infographic) and *at least three subquestions* (each of which will be addressed by your infographic’s component visualizations). Have these questions changed at all since FPM #1? If yes, how so?

Main question: In a *Turbinaria sp.*-dominated backreef in Mo'orea, which functional groups of fish are the winners and losers?

1) How have coral cover and macroalgae cover changed over time in the LTER 1 backreef?

2) How does the abundance of each functional group in 2024 compare to 2006?

3) Which functional groups are the biggest winners and losers in the LTER 1 backreef?

------------------------------

Explain which variables from your data set(s) you will use to answer the above questions, and how.

From annual fish survey data: `year`, `date`, `finetrophic`, `site`, and `habitat`
From annual backreef benthic cover data: `year`, `date`, `site`, `habitat`, `benthic_category` and `percent_cover`

Using annual fish survey data, I will filter to the LTER 1 backreef and sum fish abundance by fine trophic level and year. Using annual benthic cover data, I will filter to the same site and calculate mean percent cover for coral and macroalgae across all transects by year. 

In [FPM #2](https://eds-240-data-viz.github.io/course-materials/assignments/FPM2.html), you created some exploratory data viz to better understand your data. You may already have some ideas of how you plan to formally visualize your data, but it’s *incredibly* helpful to look at visualizations by other creators for inspiration. Find *at least two* data visualizations that you could (potentially) borrow / adapt pieces from. Download and embed them into your `drafting-viz.qmd` file, and explain which elements you might borrow (e.g. the graphic form, legend design, layout, etc.).

From: Environmental DNA reveals fine‐scale habitat associations for sedentary and resident marine species across a coastal mosaic of soft‐ and hard‐bottom habitats - Scientific Figure on ResearchGate. Available from: https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Relationships-between-eDNA-detection-rates-and-relative-fish-abundance-recorded-by-BRUVS_fig5_360615267 [accessed 22 Feb 2026]

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("insp", "eDNA.png"))
```

Ultimately, I want to use animated figures to represent changes in each functional group over time. I like how this figure facets by functional group and displays the relationship between MaxN and Amp1. I may adopt a similar approach to show abundance by year, and include a representative species illustration in each facet. I also like how each group has a distinct color scheme.

From: The evolution of fishes on coral reefs: Fossils, phylogenies, and functions - Scientific Figure on ResearchGate. Available from: https://www.researchgate.net/figure/The-six-major-phases-in-the-evolution-of-reef-fishes-The-coloured-bars-indicate-the_fig5_288518646 [accessed 22 Feb 2026]
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("insp", "evolution.png"))
```

Following the theme of the previous figure, I like the inclusion of a representative species illustration for each family alongside the color scheme. I may also borrow the stacked area layout if I choose to display all functional groups in a single panel.


### **IV. Hand-draw your anticipated visualizations**

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("insp", "hand_drawn.png"))
```


### **V. Recreate your hand-drawn visualizations using code**

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
fish <- read_csv(here::here("data", "MCR_LTER_Annual_Fish_Survey_20250324.csv")) |> 
  janitor::clean_names()
backreef <- read_csv(here::here("data", "MCR_LTER_Coral_Cover_Backreef_Long_20260219.csv"))
```


```{r}
coral_cover <- backreef |> 
  filter(benthic_category == "coral", 
         site == "LTER_1") |> 
  group_by(year, site) |> 
  summarise(mean_site_coral_cover = mean(percent_cover)) 

macroalgae_cover <- backreef |> 
  filter(benthic_category == "macroalgae", 
         site == "LTER_1") |> 
  group_by(year, site) |> 
  summarise(mean_site_algae_cover = mean(percent_cover)) 

joined_cover <- left_join(coral_cover, macroalgae_cover) |> pivot_longer(cols = c(mean_site_coral_cover, mean_site_algae_cover),
               names_to = "type",
               values_to = "cover")

ggplot(joined_cover, aes(x = year, y = cover, color = type)) + 
  geom_line() + 
  scale_color_manual(values = c("mean_site_algae_cover" = "#4a7c3f",
                                "mean_site_coral_cover" = "#e8735a"),
                     labels = c("Macroalgae", "Coral")) + 
  labs(title = "Macroalgae takes over LTER 1 backreef", 
       x = "Year", 
       y = "Mean Cover (%)", 
       color = NULL) + 
  theme_classic()
```
```{r}
# Prepare start (2006) and end (2024) counts
fish_start <- fish_2006 |> 
  rename(start = total_number) |> 
  select(fine_trophic, start)

fish_end <- fish_2024 |> 
  rename(end = total_number) |> 
  select(fine_trophic, end)

# Join and calculate change
dumbbell_data <- fish_start |> 
  left_join(fish_end, by = "fine_trophic") |> 
  mutate(change = end - start,
         fine_trophic = reorder(fine_trophic, change)) |> 
  filter(fine_trophic != "Benthic Invertebrate Consumer")

ggplot(dumbbell_data, aes(y = fine_trophic)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = start, xend = end, yend = fine_trophic), color = "grey60") +
  geom_point(aes(x = start, color = "2006"), size = 3) +
  geom_point(aes(x = end, color = "2024"), size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("2006" = "lightblue", "2024" = "navy"),
                     name = NULL) +
  labs(x = "Count", y = NULL, title = "Winners and Losers of the backreef") +
  theme_minimal()
```
```{r}
# Filter for 4 groups, sum their observations per year in backreef locations
functional_through_years <- fish |> 
  group_by(year, fine_trophic) |> 
  filter(site == "LTER_1", 
         habitat == "Backreef", 
         fine_trophic %in% c("Corallivore", "Browser", "Scraper", "Excavator")) |> 
  summarise(n = n()) |> 
  mutate(total_number = n)

# Custom palette for different functional groups
palette <- c("Browser" =  "#264653","Corallivore"=  "#2A9D8F", "Excavator" = "#E9C46A", "Scraper" = "#E76F51")

# Plot 
group_plot <- ggplot(functional_through_years, 
       aes(x = year, 
           y = total_number, 
           color = fine_trophic)) + 
  facet_wrap(~fine_trophic) + 
  geom_line() + 
  scale_color_manual(values = palette) + 
  labs(x = "Year", 
    y = "Count", 
    title = "LTER 1 Backreef Functional Group Abundance Change (2006-2024)", 
    color = "Functional Group")

group_plot
```

### **VI. Answer a few last questions about your work and progress**

What are the key insights you want your infographic to communicate, and how will your design choices help highlight and support those messages?
 
I want my infographic to communicate that this site transformed from coral-dominated to macroalgae-dominated, and that the fish community shifted accordingly. There were winners (excavators and scrapers) and losers (browsers and corallivores). The flow of my infographic is intended to tell this story sequentially, guiding the viewer from benthic change to fish community response.

2. What challenges did you encounter or anticipate encountering as you continue to build / iterate on your visualizations in R? If you struggled with mocking up any of your three visualizations, describe those challenges here.

I struggled with finding the best way to display change in abundance. I settled on a dumbbell plot because it clearly shows initial and final counts while emphasizing the direction and magnitude of change. I reordered the groups from most to least change to reinforce the winners-and-losers narrative.

3. What ggplot extension tools / packages do you need to use to build your visualizations? Are there any that we haven’t covered in class that you’ll be learning how to use for your visualizations?

I am currently only using ggplot2. I may not need additional packages beyond what we've covered in class, though I plan to export and refine my figures in Affinity Designer for final polish.

4. What feedback do you need from the instructional team and / or your peers to ensure that your intended message and key insights are clear?

I would like feedback on whether my current plots effectively communicate the intended narrative. Specifically, are the winners and losers clearly conveyed? I would also welcome suggestions on additional visual elements or design choices that could strengthen the message.
